title: createRelation does not verify toId exists or shares a project with fromId
description: POST /api/work-packages/[id]/relations only checks the caller's permission on the from-side WP, then directly calls prisma.workPackageRelation.create with the user-supplied toId. There is no verification that the target work package (parsed.toId) exists, is not the same as fromId (self-loop), or belongs to the same project as the from WP. Schema has no cross-project FK constraint, so an attacker with WORK_PACKAGE_EDIT on project A can create a relation pointing at a WP in project B. This leaks project boundaries and corrupts the data model (the assertRelationProjectMembership helper assumes relations are intra-project, and the activity subjectId is set to the from-WP's id even though the relation affects the to-WP's project too).
suggested_fix: Before creating the relation: (1) require parsed.toId !== id (reject self-loops with 400), (2) fetch the to-WP and assert it exists (404) and that toWP.projectId === fromWP.projectId (422 UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY or 400 CROSS_PROJECT_RELATION). Wrap the two reads + the create in a single transaction so the existence check can't race with the insert.

title: PATCH accepts any statusId without validating the transition is legal
description: The PATCH handler accepts any statusId in updateWorkPackageSchema and writes it to the work package with no validation. There is no StatusTransition model in schema.prisma (confirmed: zero matches for `StatusTransition|statusTransition`). Without a workflow/transition table, status can be flipped from any state to any other state (e.g., 'closed' → 'new' or 'rejected' → 'in progress'), violating OpenProject-style workflows and the closed-status invariants downstream code likely relies on. The reorder route also accepts any targetStatusId without checking it's reachable from the current status.
suggested_fix: Add a WorkPackageStatusTransition model (fromStatusId, toStatusId, role permissions) populated per-type, then in the PATCH handler look up the current statusId from the loaded workPackage, the new statusId from body, and verify a transition row exists. Return 422 INVALID_STATUS_TRANSITION if not. Apply the same check in reorder.ts when statusId is changing (or simply call the PATCH validator from reorder).

title: POST does not validate assigneeId is a member of the target project
description: createWorkPackageSchema accepts an optional assigneeId, and the handler creates the work package with that assigneeId without verifying the user is a member of the project. The only membership check is on the caller (line 158-166). This means: (1) any project member can assign work to a user who has no access to that project, leaking the WP into that user's assignment lists across the system, and (2) it bypasses the project-scoped permission model that the assertWorkPackageViewPermission helper enforces.
suggested_fix: After the project-membership check, if body.assigneeId is set, query prisma.member.findUnique({ where: { projectId_userId: { projectId, userId: body.assigneeId } } }) and reject with 422 ASSIGNEE_NOT_PROJECT_MEMBER if absent. Apply the same check in the PATCH handler at pages/api/work-packages/[id].ts when 'assigneeId' is being changed.

title: PATCH does not validate assigneeId is a member of the WP's project
description: Mirrors WP-3 for the update path. The PATCH schema accepts an optional assigneeId and writes it directly. There is no member lookup, so a non-project-member user can be assigned work packages. Note that an explicit null clears the assignee (line 26 schema allows nullable), but no transition is logged for that case either.
suggested_fix: Load the current workPackage (already done for the activity log via the `old` variable at line 127). If data.assigneeId is in the body and non-null, verify the assignee is a member of workPackage.projectId; if the body explicitly sets null, allow it. Reject 422 ASSIGNEE_NOT_PROJECT_MEMBER otherwise.

title: Date sanity refine fires only when BOTH startDate and dueDate are set, allowing start>due via partial updates
description: The zod refine at line 26-34 compares new start vs new due, but it operates on the request body alone. In the PATCH path (updateWorkPackageSchema, pages/api/work-packages/[id].ts line 32-40) the same logic applies: the refine only checks the two fields in the request body, not the effective dates after merge with the stored row. Concretely: a client can PATCH startDate='2026-12-01' on a WP whose stored dueDate is '2026-06-13' and it will be accepted, producing start > due. This violates the documented invariant the refine is trying to enforce.
suggested_fix: In updateWorkPackage at pages/api/work-packages/[id].ts, fetch the current startDate/dueDate from the existing row (the `old` variable already has the full record, lines 127). Compute effectiveStart = data.startDate !== undefined ? data.startDate : old.startDate, same for effectiveDue, and validate effectiveStart <= effectiveDue after the merge. Reject 422 INVALID_DATE_RANGE otherwise.

title: GET /api/work-packages has no pagination, no projectId default, and no project-membership filter
description: The list endpoint at line 129 calls prisma.workPackage.findMany with no `take`/`skip`, no `cursor`, and no upper bound. A user with no project membership can still hit this endpoint (no membership gate — only the withRoute HOF session check), and will get an unpaginated result across all projects in the database. The CSV branch (line 142-149) reuses the same unbounded result and streams it as a download. On a multi-tenant install this is both a data-leak vector and a denial-of-service surface (one request materializes the entire WP table). The v3 endpoint at pages/api/v3/work-packages.ts has proper pagination (lines 76-77) and so does the new /api/work-packages index for the basic case, but the v3 list and the /api/work-packages list are inconsistent — the v3 one (and the relationships in hooks/use-work-packages.ts) lacks an offset/pageSize cap below 1, allowing clients to skip negative or zero counts.
suggested_fix: (1) Add default `take: 50` and a hard cap (e.g. 200) plus `skip` from a validated `page` query param in the main GET handler. (2) Apply a project-visibility/membership filter: WHERE projectId IN (SELECT projectId FROM member WHERE userId = session.user.id) unless session.user.isSystemAdmin. (3) In the v3 handler, clamp pageSize/offset to non-negative integers, not just min(1).

title: POST create is not in a transaction; position race and orphan activity on webhook failure
description: The create flow (lines 168-211) does: (1) read max position, (2) insert WP, (3) create prisma.activity row, (4) emitActivity, (5) fire-and-forget webhook. The position read at line 168-171 and the create at line 173 are outside any transaction. Two concurrent creates for the same project can both read the same max position and end up with duplicate positions, breaking the (position, statusId) ordering relied on by the board view. Separately, the fire-and-forget dynamic import at line 214-233 has no error tracking back to the DB — a webhook that consistently fails leaves no audit row, and the error is only logged to console.
suggested_fix: Wrap the max-position read + create in prisma.$transaction with isolation Serializable, or use a single `create` that computes position via a raw upsert. For the webhook, persist a `webhook_dispatch` row in the same transaction as the WP create (with status='pending') and have a background worker consume it; log retry failures to that row instead of console.

title: PATCH /api/relations/[id] allows changing fromId/toId without verifying both WPs share a project (B-4)
description: The B-4 migration added project-membership gating for the caller, but did NOT validate that an updated fromId/toId points to a WP in the same project. The PATCH body allows changing fromId and toId (lines 13-14 of updateRelationSchema). After the update, the relation could link WPs in two different projects — violating the invariant that `assertRelationProjectMembership` resolves only `relation.from.projectId`. This silently corrupts the cross-project isolation that B-4 was trying to enforce, and the data model (no DB-level check) will accept it.
suggested_fix: In the PATCH branch, after computing the new fromId/toId, fetch the new endpoint WPs and assert: (1) both exist, (2) both are non-equal to each other (no self-loop), (3) both have projectId equal to the relation's resolved projectId (which is `relation.from.projectId` before update). Reject 422 CROSS_PROJECT_RELATION or RELATION_ENDPOINT_INVALID.

title: DELETE /api/relations/[id] does not emit activity or audit, unlike the work-package-relations route
description: The sibling handler at pages/api/work-packages/[id]/relations.ts DELETE branch (line 101-141) emits a unified activity with subjectType='work_package', subjectId=id. But pages/api/relations/[id].ts DELETE (line 91-100) does not — there is no emitActivity call, no audit log entry. Two endpoints that mutate the same entity take different code paths, so the activity timeline is incomplete depending on which endpoint clients use. Also, this route accepts DELETE on a relation whose `from` WP may be in a different project than the user has membership in: assertRelationProjectMembership uses `relation.from.projectId`, but DELETE in the work-packages/[id]/relations.ts uses `id` (the URL WP id) as the project source, so membership scope is inconsistent between the two endpoints.
suggested_fix: Add an emitActivity call in the DELETE branch at line 91-100 mirroring the sibling route. Also reconcile which project's membership is enforced: the comment at lib/auth/project.ts:379-381 says relations are intra-project, so both endpoints should call assertRelationProjectMembership rather than mixing assertWorkPackageEditPermission (work-packages/[id]/relations.ts DELETE) and assertRelationProjectMembership (relations/[id].ts).

title: Activity subjectType='work_package' is wrong for relation-created events
description: The handler comment (lines 78-82) acknowledges this: 'The activity subjectType is constrained to a known set; for work-package relations we attach via the work_package subject (relation has no direct subjectType slot, see Phase 5 schema note: types/activity.ts — fix to add 'relation' tracked separately).' The activity row is being written against the from WP only, so the to WP gets no activity entry, and downstream consumers that look up activity by subject_id on the to WP will miss this event.
suggested_fix: Add a 'work_package_relation' or 'relation' value to the activity subjectType enum, then emit a separate activity row (or two) — one keyed on the from WP, one on the to WP. Alternative: write a single activity keyed on the relation id with a generic subjectType='relation', and have the to/from consumers join via details.toId / details.fromId.

title: PATCH on non-existent WP returns 200 (no early existence check)
description: The PATCH handler at line 99 loads `wp0` at line 106-109 to check the project for RBAC, returns 404 if the WP is missing, then proceeds to parse body and call prisma.workPackage.update. However, if the WP is concurrently deleted between the existence check and the update, the Prisma call throws a P2025 error which is caught by the generic 500 handler at line 242-244. The user sees a misleading 500 instead of 404. Symmetric issue in the DELETE branch: line 274 calls prisma.workPackage.delete with no try/catch for P2025 — race-deletes produce 500s.
suggested_fix: Wrap the prisma.workPackage.update / .delete calls in try/catch and translate Prisma error code P2025 to a 404 with code 'WORK_PACKAGE_NOT_FOUND'. The PATCH already does this for the pre-check at line 106-112; the symmetric handling on the actual write is missing.

title: DELETE expects relationId in query string on a route whose path already carries the WP id
description: The DELETE branch reads `const { relationId } = req.query`. The route is `/api/work-packages/[id]/relations` — the WP id is in the path, but the relation to delete is in the query string. This is awkward: the relationship between WP and relation is implicit, and a client could pass any relationId (even one where the WP isn't either endpoint). The handler does not verify that the relation's fromId === id or toId === id, so a user with edit on WP X can delete a relation between WPs Y and Z.
suggested_fix: Either (a) move relationId to the path: `/api/work-packages/[id]/relations/[relationId]`, or (b) after loading the relation, verify relation.fromId === id || relation.toId === id, and return 400 RELATION_NOT_ATTACHED if not. Option (a) is cleaner REST and matches the per-relation PATCH/DELETE endpoint at /api/relations/[id].

title: useWorkPackageRelations lacks an isError / error state exposure
description: The hook returns the raw useQuery result which has `isLoading`/`isError`/`error` properties, but the hook wraps the work-packages list (line 142-151) and only exposes `{ workPackages }` — there is no pass-through of `isLoading`, `isError`, or `error`. Consumers cannot render an error state or loading skeleton without re-implementing the query. Compare useWorkPackage (line 154-160) which also just returns the raw useQuery result (so is fine), but the list wrapper at line 142 is restrictive.
suggested_fix: Return `{ workPackages, isLoading, isError, error, refetch }` from useWorkPackages so table/board/calendar views can show skeletons and error messages without duplicating the query call.

title: useWorkPackages enabled expression is a no-op tautology
description: Line 147: `enabled: !filters?.projectId || !!filters.projectId` — this is `!x || x` which is always true. The intent was probably to disable the query when no projectId is supplied (so the list doesn't return cross-tenant data), but the condition as written enables the query unconditionally. Combined with WP-6 (no server-side project-membership filter on the list endpoint), this means the hook always fetches all work packages in the database, then filters client-side if any filter is applied.
suggested_fix: If the intent is to require a projectId, change to `enabled: !!filters?.projectId`. Otherwise remove the line — it's misleading. Note: this is a client-side gate only; the real fix is on the server (WP-6).

title: No ErrorBoundary around work-packages view components
description: Searched 40 .tsx files under components/work-packages for ErrorBoundary or error.tsx patterns — zero matches. The board, table, calendar, and gantt views all consume useWorkPackages/useWorkPackage hooks which can throw (network errors, 403 from assertWorkPackageViewPermission, 404 WORK_PACKAGE_NOT_FOUND). Without an error boundary, a single failing fetch can unmount the whole WP page. The detail view (tested in __tests__/components/work-packages-detail.test.tsx) is particularly exposed since it fetches parent + children + watcher state on mount.
suggested_fix: Add a shared <WorkPackageErrorBoundary> wrapper (or use Next.js's app-level error.tsx pattern adapted to Pages Router via a custom <ErrorBoundary> component) around the table, board, calendar, gantt, and detail view roots. Surface the error envelope from the API (e.g. 403 FORBIDDEN → 'You don't have access to this work package') instead of a generic 'Failed to fetch' message.

title: POST does not validate typeId/priorityId/statusId belong to the target project
description: createWorkPackageSchema accepts statusId, typeId, priorityId as cuids with no check that they exist in the project's allowed values. Each project has its own status/type/priority sets (a status of 'Closed' in project A may not exist in project B, or worse, a status ID from project A could be passed to a WP in project B). Prisma will accept any valid cuid referencing a real row regardless of which project it belongs to, producing a WP that is semantically inconsistent (e.g. a 'Bug' type in a project that only allows 'Task' and 'Feature').
suggested_fix: After loading the project (or as part of the same transaction), verify that statusId, typeId, and priorityId each exist and that the project's type_ids/priority_ids/status_ids list contains them. OpenProject has explicit per-project type/priority mappings via projectType / projectPriority join tables. Return 422 INVALID_TYPE_FOR_PROJECT if not.

title: reorder accepts any targetStatusId without cross-project validation
description: The reorder handler validates that targetStatusId exists in the Status table (line 43-49), but does not check that the target status is in the same project as the WP. Statuses are global, so this is usually fine, but if a project has a constrained status set (projectStatus join), reordering to an out-of-project status will produce a WP in a state the project doesn't recognize. Symmetric to WP-16 but for the reorder path.
suggested_fix: If a per-project status mapping table exists, validate the target status is in the WP's project. Otherwise, add a TODO comment documenting the assumption that statuses are global.

title: useDeleteRelation over-invalidates: refetches all work-packages list queries
description: Line 280 invalidates `['work-packages']` (a prefix match) on every relation delete, which refetches every list query across every filter combination in every view. This is a stampede risk in a UI with many open tabs / boards. The createRelation hook (line 256-270) correctly invalidates only the two affected WP relation queries, but the delete hook has no `fromId`/`toId` context (it only has the relation id) so the author over-invalidated instead of fetching the relation first to discover the endpoints.
suggested_fix: Change `useDeleteRelation` to take the relation id, and inside the mutationFn first GET the relation to learn the fromId/toId, then invalidate the two specific workPackageRelations query keys. Or store the fromId/toId in the mutation variables (since the client knows which row was deleted, it can pass them in).

title: v3 list endpoint offset clamp is off-by-one: pageSize limit is correct but offset is silently floored to 1
description: Line 76: `const offset = Math.max(1, parseInt(req.query.offset as string) || 1)` — a client requesting offset=0 (valid OpenProject convention for the first page) silently gets offset=1, which actually returns page 2 of the result. The skip is `offset - 1` (line 96), so offset=0 maps to skip=-1, but the clamp forces it to 1, mapping to skip=0. This is logically OK for offset=1, but for offset=2 clients get skip=1 (page 1), skipping the first record. The semantics are inverted from the OpenProject v3 spec which uses 1-indexed offsets but should accept offset=0 as the first page or 1 as the first page consistently.
suggested_fix: Document the offset convention (1-indexed) and reject offset < 1 with 400. Or follow OpenProject v3 and start at 0: `const offset = Math.max(0, parseInt(req.query.offset as string) || 0); const skip = offset;`.

title: No CSRF protection or origin check on PATCH/DELETE; relies solely on session cookie
description: Standard Next.js Pages Router doesn't apply CSRF middleware to API routes by default. The PATCH/DELETE handlers (lines 99 and 247) accept any request with a valid session cookie, including cross-origin form submissions and AJAX from a malicious site. The reorder route (via withRoute) has rate limiting and the HOF's session check, but pages/api/work-packages/[id].ts is a pre-withRoute raw handler and does not enforce Origin/Referer header checks. Note: checkRateLimit is IP-based and bypassed in tests; it doesn't help with CSRF.
suggested_fix: Add an Origin/Referer allowlist check at the top of the PATCH/DELETE branches in pages/api/work-packages/[id].ts (or migrate the whole file to withRoute HOF as the TODO at line 6 already notes). NextAuth.js v5's authOptions can include a callbacks.authorized that does the origin check, but applying it in the handler is sufficient for the immediate gap.

title: assertWorkPackageViewPermission for GET but no pagination on relation list
description: The GET branch (line 36-52) returns ALL relations where fromId or toId matches the WP id, with no take/skip. A WP with thousands of relations (rare but possible in large projects) returns all in one response. Lower-severity mirror of WP-6.
suggested_fix: Add default `take: 100` and accept `page` query param for pagination. Order by relation.createdAt or a stable secondary key.
